title: Opposition Crisis Deepens, Energy Deals Stack Up at Baku Energy Week
subhead: A court ruling ousting CHP leader Ozel draws tens of thousands to Ankara as the party splits into rival camps. Meanwhile, Turkey locks in long-term gas from Azerbaijan's Absheron field and BP hands BTC pipeline operations to SOCAR — a day of political turbulence at home and energy realignment abroad.

sections.heading: CHP Leadership Crisis: Court Ousting of Ozel Sparks Mass Protests and Party Rift
sections.body: An appeals court ruling on May 21 annulled the CHP's 2023 party congress, effectively removing Ozgur Ozel as chairman and reinstating his predecessor, Kemal Kilicdaroglu. The court cited alleged irregularities in the congress vote that had elevated Ozel over the 13-year incumbent. On Saturday May 30, tens of thousands of Ozel supporters marched through central Ankara from Guven Park to Anitkabir, with Ozel declaring: 'This is not an internal matter for the CHP — this is a matter between Erdogan and the nation' (AP). The same day, Kilicdaroglu held a rival gathering at CHP headquarters, which police had stormed the previous Sunday to remove Ozel loyalists (AP, Reuters). Ozel supporters called Kilicdaroglu a 'traitor' (MSN). The CHP is currently level with the ruling AKP in most opinion polls (AP). The crisis compounds the broader pressure on the opposition: Istanbul mayor Ekrem Imamoglu has been imprisoned since March 2025 facing criminal cases that could carry decades-long sentences (AP).
sections.whyItMatters: The court ruling has effectively split Turkey's main opposition party at a time when it is polling even with the AKP. Whether Ozel reconsolidates control or the party fractures will shape whether Erdogan faces a credible challenger whenever the next election comes.

sections.heading: Economy Slows Further: Q1 GDP Growth Misses All Estimates
sections.body: Turkiye's economy grew just 2.5% year-on-year in Q1 2026, with quarterly growth at only 0.1% seasonally adjusted — below all Bloomberg estimates (The Star/Bloomberg). The central bank's tightening cycle, intensified by geopolitical risk from the Iran war, is weighing on domestic demand. Annual inflation remains above 32%, and the central bank revised its year-end targets upward in May, citing heightened geopolitical uncertainty (Xinhua). Fuel prices were cut effective June 2: gasoline fell 1.38 lira/liter and diesel 1.81 lira/liter, after Brent crude dropped to around $94.40/barrel on expectations of a potential US-Iran deal (Xinhua/Haberler). Turkiye introduced a sliding-scale fuel tax in early March to buffer consumers from geopolitical price shocks (Xinhua).
sections.whyItMatters: Below-forecast growth plus persistent inflation above 32% is a painful combination. Fuel price cuts offer short-term relief but are driven by external oil market dynamics, not domestic policy — and the central bank's upward target revision signals it expects the inflation fight to drag on.

sections.heading: Energy: Turkey Locks In 33 bcm of Azerbaijani Gas, BTC Pipeline Shifts to SOCAR
sections.body: Three energy developments converged at Baku Energy Week. First, SOCAR, TotalEnergies, ADNOC International, and Turkey's BOTAS signed a natural gas sales and purchase agreement for production from Azerbaijan's Absheron field Phase 2 (Reuters). Separately, the Turkish Energy Ministry confirmed a 15-year deal for 33 billion cubic metres of Azerbaijani gas for Turkey (MSN). Second, BP announced it will transfer BTC pipeline operations to SOCAR effective July 1, fulfilling a longstanding contractual obligation. The BTC pipeline moves over 1 million barrels/day from the Caspian to Ceyhan. BP retains a 30.1% stake; SOCAR holds 32.97% (Pipeline Journal, Reuters). Third, Turkey's northern Cyprus gas pipeline — running 97 km from Alanya to Teknecik — is expected to be operational by 2028 at a cost of ~$700 million, according to northern Cyprus 'public works minister' Erhan Arikli (Cyprus Mail).
sections.whyItMatters: The Absheron and 33-bcm deals lock in long-term supply for a country still burning expensive fuel oil in northern Cyprus. The BTC handover to SOCAR marks Azerbaijan's growing role as operator of its own energy corridors — and the Cyprus pipeline, while contentious, signals Ankara's intent to extend its energy reach into the eastern Mediterranean.

sections.heading: NATO Summit: Fidan Says Trump Expected in Ankara Next Month
sections.body: Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said President Donald Trump is expected to attend the NATO summit in Ankara next month (July 2026). The summit will be the first NATO leaders' meeting hosted in Turkey and comes at a moment of heightened alliance focus on burden-sharing and Middle Eastern security. Fidan's remarks, reported by Bloomberg and MSN, did not specify whether Trump has formally confirmed attendance. The summit venue in Ankara underscores Turkey's effort to position itself as a central NATO actor despite persistent tensions over Sweden's accession, S-400 sanctions, and Kurdish policy.
sections.whyItMatters: If Trump attends, it will be a major diplomatic moment for Erdogan — the first NATO summit on Turkish soil, with the US president present. That could reset the tone of a relationship strained by S-400 sanctions, Syria policy disagreements, and trade disputes. If he doesn't, the optics would be pointed.

sections.heading: US-Azerbaijan: $8 Billion in Commercial Deals Signed
sections.body: A US delegation signed commercial agreements worth more than $8 billion with Azerbaijan, and Washington plans to invest in the country's energy sector, according to US Assistant Secretary of State Caleb Orr (Reuters, Trend.az). The deals were announced at Baku Energy Week on June 2. A separate framework agreement on critical minerals cooperation between Azerbaijan and the US was also signed (Trend.az). Azerbaijan is now supplying gas to 16 countries, up from 12 a year ago, and 3.72 bcm flowed to Europe via TAP in January-April 2026 alone (AzerNews).
sections.whyItMatters: The $8B package signals the US is competing for influence in the Caspian energy corridor — not just leaving it to European buyers. For Turkey, deeper US-Azerbaijan ties mean its gas supply chain from Azerbaijan becomes a shared strategic interest with Washington, which could complicate relations with Iran and Russia but also create leverage.

sections.heading: Erdogan Visits Serbia, Declares 'Golden Era' in Bilateral Ties
sections.body: President Erdogan visited Belgrade and met with Serbian President Aleksandar Vucic, describing Turkey-Serbia relations as a 'golden era' (Connecting Region, TRT World). Turkish investments in Serbia have surpassed $400 million, employing nearly 96,000 people. Several agreements were signed, including memorandums on disaster management, employment, energy transition, and sports/youth/media cooperation, plus protocols linking Belgrade with Gaziantep and establishing archives cooperation (Connecting Region). Erdogan also announced plans to visit Novi Pazar — a center of the Bosniak-majority Sandzak region — with a large delegation in the coming months (Kossev).
sections.whyItMatters: The Belgrade visit reinforces Turkey's Balkans outreach at a time when EU enlargement is stalled and regional tensions over Kosovo persist. The Sandzak connection gives Erdogan cultural and religious influence beyond pure trade — and the energy transition MOU aligns Serbian decarbonization with Turkish commercial interests.

sections.heading: Ripple Launches RLUSD Stablecoin in Turkey as Crypto Market Hits $200B Annual Volume
sections.body: Ripple announced on June 2 that its USD-backed stablecoin RLUSD is now available in Turkiye through partnerships with local platforms BiLira, Bitexen, and Bitlo (CoinLaw, Finextra). RLUSD has surpassed $1.8 billion in market capitalization since its late-2024 launch. Turkiye processes nearly $200 billion in annual crypto transaction volume, making it the largest crypto market in MENA (Ripple). Ripple also announced a new University Blockchain Research Initiative partnership with Istanbul Technical University, funded through RLUSD. Separately, Reuters reported that Turkish lira-pegged stablecoins have become the second-most widely used after dollar tokens, reflecting persistent demand for inflation hedges (Reuters).
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's crypto market volume is staggering for its GDP size — driven by lira depreciation and inflation above 32%. RLUSD's entry and the rise of TRY-pegged stablecoins both reflect the same underlying pressure: Turks are seeking dollar and crypto alternatives to preserve purchasing power. Regulators will face increasing pressure to formalize oversight.

bottomLine: June 2 was defined by a deepening domestic crisis and accelerating energy realignment. The court-ordered removal of CHP leader Ozel has split Turkey's main opposition at a time when it is polling even with the AKP — tens of thousands marched in Ankara while rival factions held competing rallies. Economically, Q1 growth of 2.5% YoY missed all estimates as the central bank's tightening bites, and inflation above 32% continues to drive Turks toward crypto alternatives. On the energy front, Turkey locked in 33 bcm of Azerbaijani gas and the BTC pipeline shifted to SOCAR control, while US-Azerbaijan deals worth $8 billion underscore Washington's strategic interest in the Caspian corridor. With Trump expected at the Ankara NATO summit next month, Turkey is simultaneously navigating internal political turmoil and positioning itself at the center of an energy and security realignment that spans from Baku to Belgrade.
sources: AP, Reuters, Bloomberg, Xinhua/Haberler, Pipeline Journal, CoinLaw, Finextra, Cyprus Mail, AzerNews, Trend.az, Connecting Region, TRT World, Kossev, MSN, The Star
